intro:
Omnipresence is the perfection of God by which he is present everywhere in the fullness of his being. He is not spread thin across space like a substance, nor confined to any place, but is wholly and personally present at every point of his creation while remaining infinitely above it. “Am I a God at hand, declares the LORD, and not a God far off?… Do I not fill heaven and earth?” (Jeremiah 23:23–24).

The Old Testament presses this truth on the conscience and the heart alike. David asks, “Where shall I go from your Spirit? Or where shall I flee from your presence? If I ascend to heaven, you are there! If I make my bed in Sheol, you are there!” (Psalm 139:7–8). Solomon, dedicating the temple, marvels that “heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you, how much less this house that I have built!” (1 Kings 8:27). The fugitive cannot outrun him: “If they dig into Sheol, from there shall my hand take them” (Amos 9:2). Yet alongside this presence of his infinite being, Scripture also speaks of a special, covenantal presence of favor — God dwelling with his people in tabernacle and temple, and especially with the contrite (Isaiah 57:15). To be present everywhere in essence is not the same as to draw near in grace.

Rightly understood, omnipresence is neither pantheism nor a vague diffusion of deity. God is not his creation, and his presence does not divide him into parts; he is undivided and entire wherever he is. Nor is he present merely as power while absent in person: he is himself there. Paul tells the Athenians that God “is actually not far from each one of us, for in him we live and move and have our being” (Acts 17:27–28) — transcendent over all, yet immanent within all, sustaining every creature at every moment.

This nearness becomes most personal in Christ. The Son is Immanuel, “God with us” (Matthew 1:23), in whom the fullness of God dwelt bodily. To his gathered people he promises, “where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them” (Matthew 18:20), and at his ascension, “I am with you always, to the end of the age” (Matthew 28:20). The risen Christ now fills all things (Ephesians 4:10), and by his Spirit he makes the bodies of believers a temple of God’s indwelling presence (1 Corinthians 3:16).

For faith, the omnipresence of God is searching and comforting at once. There is no place to hide sin from him, for his eyes are in every place (Proverbs 15:3); but neither is there any place — exile, prison, deathbed, or the deep — where his sustaining hand cannot reach his own (Psalm 139:9–10). It summons the believer to reverent, watchful holiness and to fearless trust, and it points to the consummation, when the veil is removed and “the dwelling place of God is with man” forever (Revelation 21:3).
